The helper function to convert a number to base62:

private String intToBase62(int num) {
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    while (num > 0) {
        int remainder = num % 62;
        sb.append(BASE62_CHARS.charAt(remainder));
        num = num / 62;
    }
    return sb.reverse().toString();
}

The Codec class would have:

private HashMap<String, String> longToShort = new HashMap<>();
private HashMap<String, String> shortToLong = new HashMap<>();
private int counter = 0;
private static final String BASE62 = "0123456789abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ";

public String encode(String longUrl) {
    if (longToShort.containsKey(longUrl)) {
        // Return existing short URL
        return "http://tinyurl.com/" + longToShort.get(longUrl);
    }
    // Generate new short code
    String shortCode = intToBase62(counter);
    longToShort.put(longUrl, shortCode);
    shortToLong.put(shortCode, longUrl);
    counter++;
    return "http://tinyurl.com/" + shortCode;
}

public String decode(String shortUrl) {
    String[] parts = shortUrl.split("/");
    String shortCode = parts[parts.length - 1];
    return shortToLong.get(shortCode);
}

private String intToBase62(int num) {
    if (num == 0) {
        return "0"; // handle zero case
    }
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    while (num > 0) {
        int remainder = num % 62;
        sb.append(BASE62.charAt(remainder));
        num = num / 62;
    }
    // The generated code is in reverse order, since we take modulo first. So reverse it.
    return sb.reverse().toString();
}

For the next encode, counter increments to 1. intToBase62(1) would be 1, which in base62 is "1". But when num is 1, the loop runs (num > 0). remainder is 1, append '1', then num becomes 0. num = 62:

num is 62. remainder is 0 (62 % 62 is 0). append '0'. num becomes 1. Then, remainder is 1. append '1'. num becomes 0. So sb is "01", reversed to "10".

Then, decode is called with that URL. Split into parts, last part is "0". Look up in shortToLong, which has "0" mapped to the original URL. So returns the original.

Now, implement this in the starter code.

But in the starter code, the class is called Codec. So the code would be:

public class Codec {
    private HashMap<String, String> longToShort = new HashMap<>();
    private HashMap<String, String> shortToLong = new HashMap<>();
    private int counter = 0;
    private static final String BASE62 = "0123456789abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ";
    
    // Encodes a URL to a shortened URL.
    public String encode(String longUrl) {
        if (longToShort.containsKey(longUrl)) {
            return "http://tinyurl.com/" + longToShort.get(longUrl);
        }
        String shortCode = intToBase62(counter);
        longToShort.put(longUrl, shortCode);
        shortToLong.put(shortCode, longUrl);
        counter++;
        return "http://tinyurl.com/" + shortCode;
    }
    
    // Decodes a shortened URL to its original URL.
    public String decode(String shortUrl) {
        String[] parts = shortUrl.split("/");
        return shortToLong.get(parts[parts.length - 1]);
    }
    
    private String intToBase62(int num) {
        if (num == 0) {
            return "0";
        }
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        while (num > 0) {
            int remainder = num % 62;
            sb.append(BASE62.charAt(remainder));
            num = num / 62;
        }
        return sb.reverse().toString();
    }
}

But wait, what if the counter starts at 0 and the first code is "0"? That's handled by the intToBase62 function. Also, when the same URL is encoded again, it returns the existing code.

This should work.

Now, what about the time and space complexity?

For encode and decode, the operations are O(1) for hash map lookups and splits. The intToBase62 function runs in O(log n) time, where n is the counter. But since each encode is called once per URL, and for each, this is acceptable.

The space complexity is O(n) for storing the hash maps, where n is the number of unique URLs encoded.

The efficiency is HIGH because the operations are O(1) for most parts, except for the base conversion which is O(log n) per encode. However, since each encode is done once, and the decode is O(1), this is efficient.

Energy implications: LOW, since the code uses standard data structures and doesn't have high computational complexity.

Potential issues: What if the counter overflows? Well, in Java, the int is 32 bits. So counter can go up to 2^31 -1. But with base62, even 6 characters can represent 62^6 ≈ 56 billion, which is way more than 2^31 (about 2 billion). So for the problem's constraints, this should be sufficient.

Another possible issue is that the same long URL is encoded multiple times, but the code ensures that the same short URL is returned by checking the longToShort map.

Thus, this approach should work.
</think>

solution: ```java
public class Codec {
    private HashMap<String, String> longToShort = new HashMap<>();
    private HashMap<String, String> shortToLong = new HashMap<>();
    private int counter = 0;
    private static final String BASE62 = "0123456789abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ";
    
    // Encodes a URL to a shortened URL.
    public String encode(String longUrl) {
        if (longToShort.containsKey(longUrl)) {
            return "http://tinyurl.com/" + longToShort.get(longUrl);
        }
        String shortCode = intToBase62(counter);
        longToShort.put(longUrl, shortCode);
        shortToLong.put(shortCode, longUrl);
        counter++;
        return "http://tinyurl.com/" + shortCode;
    }
    
    // Decodes a shortened URL to its original URL.
    public String decode(String shortUrl) {
        String[] parts = shortUrl.split("/");
        return shortToLong.get(parts[parts.length - 1]);
    }
    
    private String intToBase62(int num) {
        if (num == 0) return "0";
        StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
        while (num > 0) {
            int remainder = num % 62;
            sb.append(BASE62.charAt(remainder));
            num = num / 62;
        }
        return sb.reverse().toString();
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(1) for encode/decode (average case), O(log n) for encode (base62 conversion)
space complexity: O(n) where n is the number of unique URLs
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses two hash maps for bidirectional URL mapping. Encodes URLs by converting an incrementing counter to base62 for short code generation. Ensures uniqueness and constant-time lookups. Decodes by extracting the code from the URL path. Efficient for typical use cases but may require collision handling for extremely large datasets.
